perturbation.original_text: Should the Investor be obliged to disclose the conﬁdential information as prescribed by law, the Investor agrees and undertakes to inform the Company in written form immediately with the speciﬁcation of the person requesting the conﬁdential information. The Investor agrees and undertakes to disclose the information within the limits deﬁned by legislation.
perturbation.changed_text: Should the Investor be obliged to disclose the conﬁdential information, the Investor agrees and undertakes to disclose the information.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the legal obligation to inform the company when disclosure is legally mandated.  It also removes the limitation that disclosure should only be 'within the limits defined by legislation'. This could violate Singaporean laws protecting trade secrets and confidential information by allowing unlimited disclosure without notice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Statutes - Section 40 of the Act - Breach of Confidence
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 40 of the Singapore Trade Secrets Act implies that unauthorized disclosure of confidential information may lead to legal ramifications, including court injunctions. The original clause offered protection in scenarios of legal disclosure, whereas the modification eliminates this protection and may expose the investor to liability.

TYSTNADSPLIKT SOM FÖLJER AV ANDRA FÖRFATTNINGAR OCH SOM INSKRÄNKER RÄTTEN ATT MEDDELA OCH OFFENTLIGGÖRA UPPGIFTER • 44 kap. Tystnadsplikt som följer av andra författningar och som inskränker rätten att meddela och offentliggöra uppgifter • Övergångsbestämmelser AVDELNING I. INLEDANDE BESTÄMMELSER 1 kap. Lagens innehåll 1 § Denna lag innehåller bestämmelser om myndigheters och vissa andra organs handläggning vid registrering, utlämnande och övrig hantering av allmänna handlingar. Lagen innehåller vidare bestämmelser om tystnadsplikt i det allmännas verksamhet och om förbud att lämna ut allmänna handlingar. Dessa bestämmelser avser förbud att röja uppgift, vare sig detta sker muntligen, genom utlämnande av allmän handling eller på något annat sätt. Bestämmelserna innebär begränsningar i yttrandefriheten enligt regeringsformen, begränsningar i den rätt att ta del av allmänna handlingar som följer av tryckfrihetsförordningen samt, i vissa särskilt angivna fall, även begränsningar i den rätt att meddela och offentliggöra uppgifter som följer av tryckfrihetsförordningen och yttrandefrihetsgrundlagen. Vad som är en allmän handling framgår av 2 kap. tryckfrihetsförordningen. Lagen består av sju avdelningar. Innehållet i dessa avdelningar är uppdelat enligt vad som framgår av 2-8 §§. 2 § Den första avdelningen innehåller inledande bestämmelser. Dessa bestämmelser avser - lagens innehåll (1 kap.), - lagens tillämpningsområde (2 kap.), och - definitioner (3 kap.). 3 § Den andra avdelningen innehåller bestämmelser om myndigheters hantering av allmänna handlingar. Dessa bestämmelser avser - hur sökande efter allmänna handlingar ska underlättas, m.m. (4 kap.), - registrering av allmänna handlinga
perturbation.justification: The original contract text ensures the company is informed when the investor is legally obligated to disclose confidential information, and it limits disclosure to what is defined by legislation. The changed text removes both requirements. The Law Explanation indicates that unauthorized disclosure of confidential information may lead to legal ramifications and that the original clause offered protection in scenarios of legal disclosure, whereas the modification eliminates this protection and may expose the investor to liability. Given this, the omission contradicts the original contract's protective measures and thus may lead to legal ramifications.

perturbation.original_text: The party who fails to encompass its obligations under the Agreement is obliged to recoup the other party for damages caused by disclosure or misuse of the conﬁdential information. The damages shall be recouped according to Singapore legislation.
perturbation.changed_text: The party who fails to encompass its obligations under the Agreement is obliged to recoup the other party for damages caused by disclosure or misuse of the conﬁdential information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'The damages shall be recouped according to Singapore legislation,' the contract becomes ambiguous regarding the legal framework governing damage recoupment.  This could lead to disputes over applicable laws and standards for calculating damages and may reduce the investor's protection under Singaporean law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Contract Law - Section 13 of the Civil Law Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Singapore Contract Law, as detailed in Section 13 of the Civil Law Act, establishes the legal basis for contractual damages. Removing the specification that damages be recouped according to Singapore legislation creates uncertainty and potential conflict with this legal framework.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Ch. 08 The Law of Contract www.singaporelawwatch.sg COMMERCIAL LAW Search Ch. 08 The Law of Contract Ch. 08A Remedies for Breach of Contract Ch. 09 Domestic Sale of Goods Ch. 10 International Sale of Goods Ch. 11 The Law of Credit and Security Ch. 12 Intellectual Property Law Ch. 13 Intellectual Property Licensing Ch. 14 Forms of Business Organisations Ch. 15 Law of Agency Ch. 16 Singapore Company Law Ch. 17 Corporate Finance and Securities Regulation Ch. 18 Equity and Trusts Ch. 19 Restitution Ch. 20 The Law of Negligence Ch. 21 Economic Torts Ch. 22 Banking and Finance Ch. 23 The Law of Guarantees Ch. 24 Insurance Law Ch. 25 Shipping Law Ch. 26 Building and Construction Law Ch. 27 Competition Law Ch. 28 Income Taxation Ch. 29 Land Law Ch. 30 Bankruptcy and Insolvency Ch. 31 Technology, Media, and Telecom No content A problem occurred while loading content. Previous Next Ch. 08 The Law of Contract SECTION 1 GENERAL APPLICATION A. Singapore contract law largely based on English contract law 8.1.1 Contract law in Singapore is largely based on the common law of contract in England. Hence, the rules developed in the Singapore courts do bear a very close resemblance to those developed under English common law. Indeed, where there is no Singapore authority specifically on point, it will usually be assumed that the position will, in the first instance, be no different from that in England. Unlike its neighbours Malaysia and Brunei, following Independence in 1965, Singapore’s Parliament made no attempt to codify Singapore’s law of contract. Accordingly, much of the law of contract in Singapore remains in the form of judge-made rules. B. Certain aspects of Singapore contract law based on specific statutes 8.1.2 In some circumstances, these judge-made rules have been modified by specific statutes. Many of these statutes are English in origin. To begin with, 13 English commercial statutes have been incorporated as part of the Statutes of the Republic of Singapore by virtue of s 4 of the Application of English Law Act (Cap 7A, 1993 Rev Ed). These are listed in Part II of the First Schedule of this Act. Other statutes, eg the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act (Cap 53B, 2002 Rev Ed), are modelled upon English statutes. There are also other areas wher
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that damages will be recouped according to Singapore legislation, providing a clear legal framework. By removing this specification, the changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the applicable legal standards for calculating and recouping damages. The Perturbation Explanation states this can lead to disputes and reduce investor protection. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states Singapore contract law is largely based on English common law but modified by specific statutes. The omission creates uncertainty, potentially conflicting with the established legal framework in Singapore, thus creating a legal contradiction by omitting a legally mandated protection.

perturbation.original_text: The Investor is authorized to grant access to the proprietary information only to those parties who require access to the proprietary information in realizing the investors’ rights under the Offer in order to achieve the provision of the Proprietary Information, are obliged not to disclose the Proprietary Information and informed about the fact of the conclusion of the Agreement. The Investor is obliged to provide the list of Investor’s Agents who have received access to the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Investor is authorized to grant access to the proprietary information to those parties who require access to the proprietary information.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the obligation for those parties to not disclose the Proprietary Information and be informed about the confidentiality agreement. It also removes the obligation for the investor to provide a list of agents with access. This significantly increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure and complicates enforcement of the NDA under Singaporean law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Statutes - Section 39 of the Act - Definition of Trade Secret
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 39 of the Singapore Trade Secrets Act defines a trade secret, which requires reasonable steps to be kept secret. Removing the requirements that authorized parties are informed of the confidentiality agreement and that a list of agents is provided weakens the protection of the trade secret under Singaporean law by creating a weaker procedure for safeguarding it.

perturbation.justification: The original text placed obligations on the investor to ensure confidentiality of proprietary information by requiring that access be limited to those who need it, that these parties are obligated not to disclose the information, and that the investor provides a list of agents with access. The changed text removes these obligations. The Law Explanation states that removing these requirements weakens the protection of the trade secret. Although neither law snippet explicitly states what the law is, the Law Explanation claims this change undermines trade secret protection under Singaporean law, because reasonable steps must be taken to keep the trade secret. This change eliminates important steps toward protecting trade secrets, therefore, a contradiction exists.
